findings.description: The original PDF uses a dotted leader line format (topic followed by dots leading to the speaker name) in a single-flow layout. The HTML converts this into a structured three-column table with borders, which is a different layout paradigm.
findings.evidence: PDF shows agenda items as lines with dotted leaders connecting topics to speakers (e.g., 'Introductions, opening remarks ........ Tom Fransen, DWR'). HTML renders this as a bordered table with Time, Topic, and Speaker columns.

findings.description: The HTML table introduces column headers 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Speaker' that do not appear in the source PDF. The PDF presents the agenda as a simple list format without explicit column headers.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <th>Time</th>, <th>Topic</th>, <th>Speaker</th>. The PDF has no such labeled columns; it uses a dotted-line format to connect topics to speakers.

findings.description: The PDF's agenda is not presented as a formal table but as a list with dotted leaders. The HTML reformats this into a structured table, which is an acceptable conversion choice but adds structural elements (column headers) not in the original.
findings.evidence: PDF uses dotted lines between topics and speakers in a list format; HTML converts to a 3-column table with headers.

findings.description: The HTML contains nested <main> elements, which is a structural duplication of the main container element.
